coredata.dc:title: Highly sensitive chemiluminescent sensor for detecting o-toluenesulfonamide and sulfamethoxazole using Cu-doped carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Xian
Xiao, Yujie
Zhao, Yue
Han, Hongping
Zheng, Hong
Zhang, Xue
coredata.dc:description: In this study, Cu doped carbon dots (Cu-CDs) of uniform particle size and good water solubility was synthesized using Angelica Sinensis Radix as precursor materials through a one-step hydrothermal. The Lucigenin-(Cu-CDs) chemiluminescence sensor allows the simultaneous determination of o-toluenesulfonamide (TFA) and sulfamethoxazole (STZ) concentrations. Under optimal conditions, the sensor demonstrates the capability to detect TFA and STZ within the ranges of 50 μM–800 μM and 15 μM–120 μM, respectively. The limits of detection (LOD) for TFA and STZ are determined as 0.09 μM and 0.05 μM, respectively. While the limits of quantification (LOQ) are established at 0.3 μM and 0.17 μM, respectively. The feasibility of the method for determining TFA and STZ content in chicken samples was substantiated, demonstrating spiked recovery rates ranging from 97.5% to 102.3 % and 97.5%–99.8 %, respectively. The possible reaction mechanism was clarified based on chemiluminescence, UV–vis measurement and free radical analysis results. The newly established system is characterized by stability, convenience, and robust anti-interference capabilities, thus expanding the application of carbon dots and offering a promising strategy for the detection of TFA and STZ.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Angelica sinensis radix
Carbon dots
Chemiluminescence
o-Toluenesulfonamide
Sulfamethoxazole

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: CL detection for TFA and STZ
CL intensity of CDs and Lucigenin system
Preparation conditions and concentration optimization of Cu-CDs
The linear response range and limit of detection
Real sample analysis of TFA and STZ

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Flow chart of TFA and STZ testing.
(a–c) TEM images of Cu-CDs at different magnifications, insets of (a) and (c) are histograms of size distribution and the HR-TEM image of Cu-CDs.
(a) Full XPS spectra of Cu-CDs and CDs; (b–d) high-resolution C 1s, O 1s and Cu 2p spectra of Cu-CDs, respectively, (e–f) high-resolution C 1s and O 1s spectra of CDs, respectively.
(a) FTIR spectra, (b) XRD spectra, and (c) Raman spectra of Cu-CDs and CDs.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of Cu-CDs at different excitation wavelengths (Ex = 300–380 nm), (b) UV–Vis spectra, fluorescence excitation spectra and emission spectra of Cu-CDs, the inset of (b) shows the photographs of Cu-CDs solutions under visible light (left) and 365 nm UV light (right).

CL emission–time curves at different concentrations of TFA(a), STZ (b), linear calibration curve for the detection of TFA(c), STZ (d), ΔI=I–I0, I and I0 represent the CL intensity in the presence and absence of TFA&STZ, respectively.
(a) UV–Vis of different systems; (b) Effect of different radical scavengers on the CL intensity of the system.
Effect of common interfering substances on the determination of TFA and STZ.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The ratio of concentration between the interfering substance and TFA&STZ, i.e. [ion]/[ TFA&STZ].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recoveries obtained for the TFA and STZ in chicken samples.
Comparison of TFA and STZ determinations using the proposed and reference methods.

head.ce:title: Highly sensitive chemiluminescent sensor for detecting o-toluenesulfonamide and sulfamethoxazole using Cu-doped carbon dots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Center of Active Substance Biotechnology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, Cu doped carbon dots (Cu-CDs) of uniform particle size and good water solubility was synthesized using Angelica Sinensis Radix as precursor materials through a one-step hydrothermal. The Lucigenin-(Cu-CDs) chemiluminescence sensor allows the simultaneous determination of o-toluenesulfonamide (TFA) and sulfamethoxazole (STZ) concentrations. Under optimal conditions, the sensor demonstrates the capability to detect TFA and STZ within the ranges of 50 μM–800 μM and 15 μM–120 μM, respectively. The limits of detection (LOD) for TFA and STZ are determined as 0.09 μM and 0.05 μM, respectively. While the limits of quantification (LOQ) are established at 0.3 μM and 0.17 μM, respectively. The feasibility of the method for determining TFA and STZ content in chicken samples was substantiated, demonstrating spiked recovery rates ranging from 97.5% to 102.3 % and 97.5%–99.8 %, respectively. The possible reaction mechanism was clarified based on chemiluminescence, UV–vis measurement and free radical analysis results. The newly established system is characterized by stability, convenience, and robust anti-interference capabilities, thus expanding the application of carbon dots and offering a promising strategy for the detection of TFA and STZ.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: O-Toluenesulfonamide (TFA) is a crucial precursor in the synthesis of various drugs, including chloramine-T, sulfathiazole and chloramphenicol. Additionally, it serves as a vital raw material for producing plasticizers, binders, colorants and synthetic fibers [1,2]. Sulfamethoxazole (STZ), an additional sulfonamide antibiotic, is primarily used as a food additive and for the treatment of urinary tract infections. It finds widespread application in veterinary and industrial livestock farming [3]. Similar to other antibiotics, TFA and STZ are acknowledged as contaminants in water and food, posing a threat to local livelihoods. Notably, STZ has been associated with hematologic abnormalities in humans. Therefore, the analysis and determination of TFA and STZ in animal residues, biological fluids and the environment ahold paramount importance [4]. Different methods have been used for the detection of STZ and TFA, including and not limited to electrochemical [5], light-assisted electroanalysis [6], spectroscopy [7], voltammetry [8], liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) [9], and reversed-phase high-performance liquid chromatography [10]. Although these methods offer high accuracy, complexity, expensive equipment, and stringent personnel requirements render them unsuitable for rapid TFA and STZ detection. Consequently, there is a crucial need for the development of a simple, fast, and economical method for TFA and STZ analysis.

Among an array of analytical methods, chemiluminescence (CL) manifests inherent advantages, such as simplicity, rapid detection, cost-effectiveness and high sensitivity. It has found widespread applications in areas such as pharmaceutical chemistry, food safety and environmental monitoring [11]. N, N′-dimethyl-9,9′-biacridinium dinitrate, colloquially recognized as Lucigenin, stands as a classical luminescent reagent with excellent selectivity and high sensitivity. CL detection typically requires the involvement of a catalyst and nanomaterials are excellent choice for this purpose. Carbon dots (CDs) are a type of emerging carbon nanomaterials with outstanding photo-luminescent properties, tunable chemical properties, high biocompatibility and low toxicity. They have been widely applied in catalysis [12], sensors [13], chemiluminescence [14], electrochemiluminescence [15], and electroanalytical [16]. Additionally, the ability to obtain CDs from biomass or organic waste has significantly considerable interest among researchers in these carbon-based nanoparticles [17,18]. Although CDs have been applied to CL systems by different researchers, the application of CDs synthesized from biomass materials to CL systems is rarely reported.

Angelica Sinensis Radix, the dried root of the apiaceae plant Angelica Sinensis Radix, possesses various medicinal properties such as promoting blood circulation, regulating menstruation, alleviating pain and moisturizing the intestines for bowel movement. It stands as a historically significant and extensively employed medicinal herb with dual purposes as food [19]. The chemical composition of Angelica Sinensis Radix encompasses polysaccharides, organic acids, volatile oils, amino acids, nucleosides and trace elements. These complex components not only exhibit diverse pharmacological effects with significant clinical value but also have the capability to initiate chemical reactions.

In this study, four types of CDs were synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal method using Angelica Sinensis Radix as the precursor and doped with different elements. Among these, Cu-doped CDs (Cu-CDs) exhibited optimal CL properties. Cu-CDs can enhance the CL strength of the Lucigenin system, resulting in a new Lucigenin-(Cu-CDs) system. Moreover, STZ and TFA further enhanced the CL intensity of the Lucigenin-(Cu-CDs) system with the degree of enhancement was linearly related to the concentrations of STZ and TFA. Upon this, a novel flow injection CL (FI-CL) method was designed for the detection of STZ and TFA. The established method is characterized by stability, convenience and exhibits strong anti-interference capabilities, expanding the application of CDs. It addresses the challenge of limited TFA detection methods and provides a new approach for the detection of other sulfonamide drugs.

Angelica Sinensis Radix was purchased from the Traditional Chinese Medicine Market (Chongqing, China). STZ and TFA (Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd). NaOH (Chongqing Chuandong Chemical (Group) Co., Ltd). Lucigenin, CuO and ZnO (Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd, China). H2O2 (Chengdu Kelong Chemical Co., Ltd, China). Chicken samples were purchased from the local market (Chongqing, China). All chemical reagents are analytical purity.

Using the Tecnai G2 F30 field emission transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI, USA) at 300 KV, the morphology and lattice structure of CDs were obtained. The ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Thermo Fisher, USA) was employed to scan the elements C, O and Cu. The binding energy of C 1s was adjusted to 284.8 eV to correct for the charging effect. The Nicolet iS10 Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Thermo Fisher, USA) was utilized to analyze the functional groups of CDs within the spectral range of 4000-400 cm−1. The D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, GER) was used to acquire XRD patterns with a diffraction range of 10°–80° and a scanning rate of 5°/min. The Raman spectrometer (Renishaw, UK) was employed to collect Raman spectra in the range of 100–3500 cm−1 with an excitation wavelength (λ) of 532 nm and measurements were conducted in parallel three times. The IFFM-D flow injection CL analyzer (IFFM-D, Remex, CHN) recorded the CL signals under 700 V high voltage and a pump speed of 30 r/min. The TU-1810DSPC UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Puxi, CHN) recorded UV–Vis spectra in the range of 200∼650 nm. The F-7100 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, JPN) conducted fluorescence spectrum (FL) detection under a 150 W xenon lamp.

Dissolve 1 g of Angelica Sinensis Radix powder in 50 mL of water and sonicate for 40 min. Subsequently, seal the mixed solution in a reaction vessel and react at 190 °C in an electric heating air-blowing drying oven for 5 h. Upon completion of the reaction, centrifuge the collected liquid at 12000 rpm for 15 min, retain the supernatant and filter it multiple times through a 0.22 μm membrane filter to remove large particles and insoluble materials. Finally, dialyze the solution using a dialysis bag for 24 h to obtain the product, referred to as CDs. Additionally, separately introduce 0.1 g of CuO, 0.1 g of ZnO and a combination of 0.1 g of CuO and 0.1 g of ZnO into the sonicated Angelica Sinensis Radix solution. Keep the other procedures unchanged. The resulting products are named Cu-CDs, Zn-CDs and Cu, Zn-CDs, respectively. The preparation processes for the four types of CDs are illustrated in Fig. S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CL detection for TFA and STZ
ce:section.ce:para: A CL system consisting of Cu-CDs, NaOH, Lucigenin and H2O2 was utilized for the detection of TFA and STZ. The instrument's pump speed was set at 30 r/min and the photomultiplier tube voltage was maintained at 700 V. After adjusting the flow paths of the system, the detection process for TFA and STZ was established (as shown in Fig. 1 ): Pump 1 was utilized for the continuous delivery of H2O2, Lucigenin and NaOH solutions, while Pump 2 was used to deliver the Cu-CDs solution and the test samples. The solutions from both systems converged at the mixer, entered the detector, underwent a reaction and generated a stable baseline CL signal denoted as I0. Subsequently, the sample injection valve was switched, allowing the TFA or STZ sample solution to enter the flow path through a secondary pump for analysis. The resulting CL signal was denoted as I and the relative CL intensity was calculated as the peak height (ΔI=I–I0).

ce:section.ce:section-title: CL intensity of CDs and Lucigenin system
ce:section.ce:para: The incorporation of heteroatoms through doping has the capacity to modify the structure of CDs, yielding CDs endowed with more pronounced luminescent properties. The obtained four types of CDs were individually added to the Lucigenin system and CL signals were detected. The results in Fig. S2(a) revealed that doped with Cu and Zn could enhance the CL signal of CDs, indicating that Cu and Zn could effectively modify the structure of CDs. However, the simultaneous doping of Cu and Zn was less effective than doping with Cu alone. This phenomenon could be attributed to a competitive effect when both elements are present simultaneously, leading to a reduction in modification efficiency and consequently resulting in a less significant CL signal. Therefore, Cu-CDs was selected to establish a new CL system in combination with Lucigenin.

Lucigenin is a classical acridinium ester-type luminescent reagent known for its high luminescent performance. Under alkaline conditions, it can be oxidized by certain strong oxidants, producing N-methylacridone (NMA) and emitting energy. However, due to the poor water solubility of NMA, it is necessary to add small amounts of surfactants or catalysts in practical chemical reactions to enhance the luminescent intensity of Lucigenin reactions [20]. As shown in Fig. S2b, Cu-CDs have certain catalytic properties, and in the presence of Cu-CDs, a novel Lucigenin-(Cu-CDs) system was established with significantly enhanced luminescence intensity.

The morphology and particle size distribution of the Cu-CDs were studied by field emission transmission electron microscopy (TEM). TEM images of Cu-CDs at different magnifications are depicted in Fig. 2 . Observation of Fig. 2(a) and (b) reveals that the synthesized Cu-CDs exhibit approximately spherical particles with good dispersion and no apparent aggregation. The particle size distribution histogram (insert in Fig. 2 a) reveals that the size of Cu-CDs ranges from 1 to 8 nm, with an average particle size of 4.36 nm. The majority of Cu-CDs particles are concentrated in the range of 3–5 nm. High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM, Fig. 2 c) displays distinct lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, primarily attributed to the (100) lattice plane of graphite. This suggests that the synthesized Cu-CDs possess a structure similar to graphite [21].

The elemental composition of Cu-CDs and CDs was verified through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. 3 (a) presents the XPS survey spectra for Cu-CDs and CDs. The XPS analysis of Cu-CDs reveals two dominant peaks at 284.8 eV and 532.1 eV, along with a weaker peak at 927.5 eV, corresponding to C 1s, O 1s and Cu 2p, respectively. The XPS survey spectrum of CDs displays two characteristic peaks at 284.8 eV and 532.6 eV, corresponding to C 1s and O 1s. Further characterization of the specific chemical structure and potential surface states of Cu-CDs and CDs was achieved through high-resolution XPS spectra. The C 1s spectrum of Cu-CDs (Fig. 3 b) can be deconvoluted into five peaks at 289.4 eV, 288.4 eV, 287.1 eV, 285.7 eV and 284.6 eV, attributed to O–CO, OC–O/N, CO/CN, C–O and C–C/CC, respectively [22–24]. The high-resolution O 1s peaks at 535.4 eV and 532.3 eV in Cu-CDs (Fig. 3 c) correspond to C–O and CO/NO [24,25]. The Cu 2p spectrum of Cu-CDs (Fig. 3 d) confirms the successful doping of Cu into the material, showing two main peaks at 936.6 eV and 956.4 eV corresponding to the Cu 2p2/3 and Cu 2p1/2 spin-orbit orbitals. Additionally, two distinct satellite peaks are observed in the ranges of 930 eV–940 eV and 950 eV–960 eV, indicating the presence of Cu. Two characteristic satellite peaks at 932.6 eV and 952.3 eV are observed, corresponding to Cu2+ [23,26]. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum of CDs (Fig. 3 e) exhibits three characteristic peaks at 288.1 eV, 285.9 eV and 284.6 eV, associated with CO, C–O and C–C/CC [27,28]. The high-resolution O 1s spectrum of CDs (Fig. 3 f) displays two peaks corresponding to O–CO and CO/NO at 534.1 eV and 532.5 eV, respectively [24,28].

The XPS results of Cu-CDs confirm the presence of functional groups such as –OH and –COOH on the surface. These oxygen-containing groups lead to the formation of numerous defects on the surface of Cu-CDs, consistent with the Raman spectroscopy. Simultaneously, the abundance of these oxygen-containing groups serves as further evidence of the outstanding water solubility exhibited by Cu-CDs. The presence of C–C bonds confirmed the good conductivity of Cu-CDs, while the CO bonds ensure close contact between the oxide and Cu-CDs. The existence of Cu2+ imparts strong chelation capabilities to Cu-CDs.

The surface chemical groups of the materials were further investigated using Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). The FTIR spectrum (Fig. 4 a) reveals distinct peaks at approximately 3270 cm−1, 2939 cm−1, 1404 cm−1 and 1042 cm−1 for Cu-CDs and CDs. These peaks are attributed to the vibrations of O–H, C–H, C–N and C–O, respectively [23,29]. Notably, peaks at 1620 cm−1 and 505 cm−1 in Cu-CDs represent the stretching vibrations of CO [24] and Cu–O bonds [30], respectively. For CDs, an observable peak at 1639 cm−1 suggests the presence of CC [31]. The FTIR results indicate the existence of chemical groups such as –COOH, –OH and –NH on the surfaces of both Cu-CDs and CDs. These findings align with the XPS results.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) was employed to reveal the crystalline phases and composition information of Cu-CDs and CDs. The results in Fig. 4(b) show that Cu-CDs and CDs exhibit a broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 22.5°, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphite. The broadening of this diffraction peak is attributed to the disordered amorphous carbon phase [30,32]. Additionally, the XRD spectrum of CDs reveals a minor peak centered at 2θ = 29.2°, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphite [25].

Raman spectroscopy was employed to further elucidate the structure of the CDs. As shown in Fig. 4(c), both Cu-CDs and CDs exhibit two main peaks around 1370 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1, corresponding to the disordered D-band and graphite G-band, respectively. The peaks of the D-bands and G-bands are commonly associated with sp3 and sp2 hybridized carbon structures. The intensity of the G-band in Cu-CDs surpasses that of the D-band, indicating that Cu-CDs are predominantly constituted of sp2 carbon, with some sp3 hybridized carbon, suggesting that Cu-CDs mainly consist of sp2 graphite carbon with sp3 carbon defects [33]. The ratio of the intensities of the D and G bands (ID/IG) can be calculated to assess the relative contributions of the graphite core state and disordered surface states to the structure [34]. The ID/IG value for Cu-CDs is 0.98, indicating the presence of surface defects, possibly related to the abundance of oxygen-containing groups on the surface of Cu-CDs. The ID/IG value for CDs is 1.20, higher than the ID/IG value for Cu-CDs, indicating a higher degree of disorder in CDs and suggesting that CDs are a lower-quality graphite material [32].

Fluorescence (FL) and UV–Vis spectroscopic analyses were conducted to explore the optical properties of Cu-CDs. To ascertain that the synthesis of Cu-CDs exhibits excitation-dependent emission characteristics, fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at different excitation wavelengths. The results in Fig. 5 (a) show that as the excitation wavelength varies from 300 nm to 400 nm, the maximum emission wavelength of Cu-CDs shifts from 390 nm to 470 nm. This phenomenon confirms the typical excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior of CDs, consistent with previous reports on biomass-based CDs [35]. Fig. 5(b) demonstrates that the maximum emission peak of Cu-CDs is observed at 395 nm under an excitation wavelength of 320 nm. As shown in Fig. 5(b), the UV–Vis results reveal a distinct absorption peak for Cu-CDs near 280 nm, attributed to the π→π* transition of the aromatic sp2 structural domains [36]. The inset in Fig. 5(b) illustrates that the aqueous solution of Cu-CDs appears brownish-yellow and transparent under natural light (left inset) but exhibits a prominent blue emission under ultraviolet light (365 nm) (right inset).

Cu-CDs were subjected to room temperature exposure for 0, 3, 6, 12, 24 and 48 h, undergoing CL testing to scrutinize the short-term stability of Cu-CDs. Additionally, Cu-CDs were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C and their status was recorded every 30 days for a continuous observation period of 90 days, documenting their CL performance to assess long-term stability. The results in Fig. S3(a) show that after being placed at room temperature for 0–48 h, the CL intensity of Cu-CDs is nearly identical to freshly prepared Cu-CDs, indicating excellent short-term stability. Fig. S3(b) reveals that after 30 days of storage, the CL intensity of Cu-CDs exhibits no significant difference from freshly prepared Cu-CDs. However, after 60 and 90 days of storage, a slight decrease in CL intensity is observed, although the decline is minimal. The appearance of Cu-CDs after 30, 60 and 90 days of storage reveals no discernible distinctions compared to freshly prepared Cu-CDs (as shown in the inset in Fig. S3b), indicating good long-term stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation conditions and concentration optimization of Cu-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of Cu-CDs involves a reaction at high temperatures for several hours. To obtain Cu-CDs with optimal CL performance, the preparation time and temperature were systematically optimized. The reaction temperature was maintained at 190 °C and the reaction duration was adjusted, followed by CL measurements of the obtained products. The results depicted in Fig. S4(a) indicate that the Cu-CDs exhibits the strongest CL signal when the reaction duration reaches 5 h. Therefore, it is recommended to control the reaction time at 5 h for the preparation of Cu-CDs.

Temperature significantly influences the performance of Cu-CDs, leading to observable differences in CL intensity under different temperature conditions. Maintaining a constant reaction duration of 5 h, the optimal preparation temperature for Cu-CDs was investigated in the range of 170 °C–210 °C, as illustrated in Fig. S4(b). As the reaction temperature increases, the CL intensity of Cu-CDs gradually rises. When the temperature reaches 190 °C, the prepared Cu-CDs exhibit the strongest CL signal. Further increasing the reaction temperature leads to a decline in the CL signal, indicating that the optimal reaction temperature is 190 °C.

As a crucial reagent for constructing the Lucigenin-(Cu-CDs) system, the concentration of Cu-CDs directly influences the system's CL signal. The obtained Cu-CDs were diluted to various concentrations and their CL signals were examined, as shown in Fig. S4(c). With an increase in dilution factors, the concentration of Cu-CDs decreases, while the CL signal continuously strengthens. The highest CL intensity of the Lucigenin-(Cu-CDs) system is observed when Cu-CDs undergo a 400-fold dilution. Further dilution results in a decline in the CL signal. Therefore, Cu-CDs subjected to a 400-fold dilution were selected as the system solution.

In an alkaline medium, the Lucigenin has capability to produce a stronger CL signal. Consequently, the concentration of NaOH plays a crucial role in influencing the CL intensity of the system. To determine the optimal NaOH concentration for TFA detection, while keeping other experimental parameters constant, investigated the influence of NaOH on the CL signal within the range of 0.1 mM–5 mM, as shown in Fig. S5(a). The CL signal for detecting TFA gradually increases as the NaOH concentration increases from 0.1 mM to 2 mM, reaching a maximum at 2 mM. However, when the NaOH concentration exceeds 2 mM, the CL signal begins to decrease gradually. Therefore, the optimal NaOH concentration for detecting TFA is determined to be 2 mM.

The influence of NaOH concentration on CL intensity for detecting STZ was explored over a range from 0.05 mM to 0.4 mM. As depicted in Fig. S5(d), the CL intensity for detecting STZ exhibits a gradual increase with rising NaOH concentration. The CL intensity was highest at a NaOH concentration of 0.2 mM. Nevertheless, a gradual decrease in the CL signal is observed with successive increases in NaOH concentration. Therefore, a NaOH concentration of 0.2 mM is selected for subsequent STZ detection.

Lucigenin concentration directly influences the CL intensity. Fig. S5(b) illustrates the CL results for detecting TFA under different Lucigenin concentrations. The graph indicates that the relative CL intensity for detecting TFA sharply increases with an escalation in Lucigenin concentration. The maximum CL intensity is achieved when the Lucigenin concentration reaches 30 μM. However, when the Lucigenin concentration exceeds 30 μM, the obtained CL signal gradually weakens. Therefore, Lucigenin at a concentration of 30 μM is deemed the optimal concentration for TFA detection.

The optimal concentration of Lucigenin for detecting STZ was investigated across the range of 4 μM–16 μM. As shown in Fig. S5(e), the highest CL response was achieved when detecting STZ under conditions with 10 μM Lucigenin. Therefore, the recommended concentration for Lucigenin during STZ detection is 10 μM.

The concentration of H2O2 has a significant impact on the luminescence intensity as an oxidant in the CL reaction. The impact of different H2O2 concentrations (ranging from 0.1 mM to 1.6 mM) on the CL signal during TFA detection is illustrated in Fig. S5(c). As the H2O2 concentration increases, the CL signal for TFA detection experiences a pronounced ascent, underscoring a notable reliance on H2O2 for effective TFA detection. The maximum CL intensity is achieved at an H2O2 concentration of 1 mM. Further elevation of the H2O2 concentration leads to the suppression of the CL signal. Consequently, for TFA detection, a concentration of 1 mM for H2O2 is recommended.

Similarly, the CL signal for STZ detection is influenced by the concentration of H2O2. The data in Fig. S5(f) indicated that at low H2O2 concentrations, the CL signal for detecting STZ is weak. The CL signal increases as the concentration of H2O2 increases, peaking at 6 mM. However, a further increase in H2O2 concentration leads to a gradual decrease in the CL signal. Therefore, for detecting STZ, it is recommended to use an H2O2 concentration of 6 mM.

As shown in Fig. S6, the chemiluminescence kinetic profile of the Lucigenin system was recorded using static injection method under optimum experimental conditions. The Lucigenin CL system alone produced weak CL signal, and the addition of Cu-CDs sensitized the Lucigenin system to produce a stronger CL signal. The CL intensity was again enhanced by the injection of TFA or STZ into the Lucigenin-(Cu-CDs) system, and reached the highest value within 1 s with a very rapid response. These results indicate that TFA and STZ can effectively enhance the CL strength of the Lucigenin-(Cu-CDs) system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The linear response range and limit of detection
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above results, a series of standard solutions containing trans fatty acids and STZ were determined under optimized experimental conditions Fig. 6 (a) and (b). The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) were calculated according to the IUPAC definitions (LOD = 3SD/K, LOQ = 10SD/K, where SD represents the standard deviation of the arithmetic mean derived form 11 readings under the same conditions and K denotes slope of the standard curve). The results in Fig. 6(c) and (d) show that the linear ranges for TFA and STZ are 50 μM–800 μM and 15 μM–120 μM, respectively. The regression equations are ΔI = 9.37×C +926.29 (R2=0.9993) for TFA and ΔI = 61.6×C-227.92 (R2=0.9995) for STZ, where C is the concentration of SFZ and TFA in μM. The LOD values are 0.09 μM and 0.05 μM, while the LOQ values are 0.3 μM and 0.17 μM for TFA and STZ, respectively. Through the consecutive measurement of a standard solution of 0.3 mM TFA and 0.05 mM STZ eleven times, the relative standard deviations (RSD) for TFA and STZ were found to be 0.92 % and 2.08 %, respectively.

Under the optimized experimental conditions, typical interferents commonly encountered in the detection of TFA and STZ were systematically introduced to evaluate interference effects in the detection of 0.3 mM TFA and 0.05 mM STZ. The interference study included Zn2+, Cu2+, K+, Na+, Ca2+, Fe3+, NH4 +, BrO3 −, Cl−, SO3 2−, C2O4 2−, NO2 −, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, HCO3 −, HCOO−, H2PO4 −, HPO4 2−, PO4 3−, Lys, Arg, Gly, soluble starch, glucose and citric acid (CA). The results of the interference study are presented in Table 1 . The results of the interference study indicated the feasibility and stability of the method for the detection of TFA and STZ in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real sample analysis of TFA and STZ
ce:section.ce:para: The effectiveness of the newly devised system was assessed though the detection of TFA and STZ content in samples of biological origin. Homogeneous samples comprising 10 g ground chicken meat were weighed, followed by the addition of 10 mL of ethyl acetate. Subsequent to a 5-min stirring period for extraction, 2 mL 0.1 M HCl was added to the extraction solution. The mixed liquid was subjected to rotary evaporated, yielding an approximate volume of 2 mL, subsequently followed by the addition of 1 mL HCl and 2 mL hexane. The combined solution underwent vortexed for a duration of 30 s, followed by centrifuged at 12,000 r/min for 5 min. The hexane layer was discarded, retaining the lower layer solution, subsequently subjected to filtration utilizing a 0.22 μm filter membrane to obtain a sample solution suitable for detection [37]. Under optimized conditions, CL measurements were executed on various groups of prepared chicken meat samples, supplemented with the addition of 0.3 mM standard TFA. The results indicated that the concentration values for each sample group ranged from 0.29 mM to 0.31 mM. Furthermore, CL measurements were conducted on various sets of prepared chicken meat samples, augmented with 50 μM standard STZ. The results showed concentration values ranging from 48.9 μM to 51.2 μM for each sample, preliminarily suggesting the absence of TFA and STZ residues in the chicken meat samples. Various concentrations of standard TFA and STZ were added to each sample, with subsequent scrutiny of recovery rates. The results are presented in Table 2 , the recovery rates for TFA ranged from 97.5 % to 102.3 %, while for STZ, the recovery rates ranged from 97.5 % to 99.8 %. This demonstrated the feasibility of the established method, which can be successfully applied to determine the TFA and STZ in real sample.

Currently, various methods have been devised to ascertain the presence of STZ, whereas the reported methods for detecting TFA remain constrained, with LC-MS/MS emerging as one of the available modalities [9]. An evaluation comparing the recently established method in this study with other analogous methods from the literature is presented in Table 3 . The data indicate that, in comparison with other previously reported methods, the newly developed method in this study exhibits specific advantages concerning the linear range, LOD and LOQ.

To explore potential luminescent mechanisms, UV–Vis tests were conducted on various systems under identical conditions. The results depicted in Fig. 7 (a) reveal distinct absorption peaks for Lucigenin at 360 nm and 430 nm. In the Lucigenin-NaOH-H2O2 system, additional peaks at 490 nm become apparent alongside those at 360 nm and 430 nm, which originate from N-methylacridone* (NMA*). Upon the addition of Cu-CDs to the Lucigenin-NaOH-H2O2 system, the absorption peaks at 360 nm and 430 nm remain unchanged, while the 490 nm peak disappears. This implies a gradually diminishment of NMA* with the introduction of Cu-CDs. Additionally, a novel absorption peak appears at 290 nm, possibly arising from the excited state of Cu-CDs (Cu-CDs*), indicating that Cu-CDs can react with NMA* and the luminescent species transitions from NMA* to Cu-CDs*. Consequently, it is evident that Cu-CDs serve not only as catalysts within the system but also function as luminescent specie. In the detection of TFA through the Lucigenin-NaOH–H2O2–(Cu-CDs) system, the originally observed absorption peak at 430 nm shifts to 410 nm. Similarly, during the detection of STZ through the Lucigenin-NaOH–H2O2–(Cu-CDs) system, the absorption peak at 430 nm undergoes a shift to 420 nm. This indicates that both TFA and STZ undergo chemical reactions within the Lucigenin-(Cu-CDs) system, resulting in the formation of intermediate products.

To investigate the generated intermediate, the influence of free radical scavengers such as ascorbic acid (Vc), thiourea (TU), nitrotetrazolium blue chloride (NBT) and l-tryptophan (L-Try) on the CL intensity of the Lucigenin-(Cu-CDs)-TFA system and Lucigenin-(Cu-CDs)-STZ system was studied. Vc serves as a classical reducing agent and a widely employed free radical scavenger in antioxidant studies. The results in Fig. 7(b) illustrate a substantial reduction on CL intensity upon the addition of 1 mM Vc to both the Lucigenin-(Cu-CDs)-TFA and Lucigenin-(Cu-CDs)-STZ systems, this observation verifies that the CL reaction proceeds through a free radical pathway. TU act as a potent scavenger for •OH. When the addition of 1 mM TU to the system, a rapid decrease in CL signal is observed, confirming the existence of •OH in the CL system. NBT widely use as the detect •O2 − free radicals, it undergoes reduction by •O2 −, resulting in the formation of a deep blue formazan. Upon the introduction of 0.1 mM NBT into the reaction system, a notable decrease in CL intensity is evident, affirming the participation of •O2 − in the reaction. L-Try functions as a scavenger for 1O2 and the discerned quenching effect in the presence of 0.1 mM L-Try suggests the presence of 1O2 in the CL reaction [38,39].

Upon to the presented findings, we proffer a plausible luminescent mechanism: Lucigenin manifests robust CL intensity exclusively in alkaline conditions. Absent the presence of Cu-CDs, as Lucigenin undergo reaction with H2O2 in an alkaline milieu, it begets an intermediate species know as dioxetane (DXT). The precarious intermediate undergoes decomposition, giving rise to the excited state of NMA*, subsequently reverting to the ground state of NMA, thereby culminating in the emission of CL signals [40]. In the presence of Cu-CDs, Lucigenin undergo reaction with H2O2 in alkaline conditions, with Cu-CDs serving as catalysts, amplifying the CL signal. In this process, reactive oxygen intermediates including •OH, •O2 − and 1O2 emerge. The enhancement of the CL signal within the Lucigenin system by Cu-CDs is attributed to the generation of oxygen-containing intermediates. These intermediates accelerate the oxidation reaction of Lucigenin, generating more NMA*. NMA* transfers energy through CRET (Chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer) to Cu-CDs and Cu-CDs* eventually transition back to the ground state, completing the release of CL.

Upon the introduction of TFA or STZ into the system, their interaction with Lucigenin induces a diminution in DXT formation, consequently resulting in a reduction of NMA* production. In this scenario, •OH and O2 − generated within the system undergo direct interactions with Cu-CDs, leading to the formation of Cu-CDs•+ and Cu-CDs•– through hole injection and electron injection processes. Subsequently, Cu-CDs* are produced through an electron transfer quenching process. Simultaneously, 1O2 directly transfers its energy to Cu-CDs*, reducing the energy transfer process. This facilitates the generation of a greater quantity of Cu-CDs* within the same temporal framework. Upon the return of Cu-CDs* to the ground state, they emit potent photons, culminating in an augmentation of CL intensity. The possible CL mechanism is delineated in Schema 1 .

In this study, Angelicae Sinensis Radix served as a green carbon source and a one-step hydrothermal method was employed to synthesize Cu-CDs with a homogeneous uniform particle distribution, stable properties and excellent water solubility. The Cu-CDs, enhanced through the incorporation of the metallic element Cu, exhibited outstanding optical properties. The Lucigenin-(Cu-CDs) system formed in conjunction with Lucigenin, manifested a significant CL signal. Consequently, a chemical luminescence sensor was developed, paving the way for a novel approach to detect TFA and STZ. Utilizing this methodology, the detection range for TFA spanned from 50 μM to 800 μM, with a LOD of 0.09 μM and a recovery rate ranging from 97.5% to 102.3 %. For STZ, the detection range extended from 15 μM to 120 μM, exhibiting an LOD of 0.05 μM and a recovery ranging from 97.5% to 99.8 %. The newly established system showcased elevated sensitivity, excellent selectivity and prompt detection capabilities. This study provides a new method for the accurate detection of TFA and STZ, which is of great practical significance and provides useful references and insights for the monitoring and control of other veterinary drug residues in food of animal origin.

Xian Yang: Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yujie Xiao: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation. Yue Zhao: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Hongping Han: Methodology, Investigation. Hong Zheng: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Xue Zhang: Supervision, Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the Science and Technology Research Program of the Chongqing Municipal Education Commission (No. KJZD-K202300504) and the Innovation and Central Nervous System Drug Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province (Grant No. 230041-01SZ)
